refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to reduce dependency on centralized decision points and improve fault isolation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) subsystem with real-time diagnostics and automated recovery procedures.
DP7: Implement configuration management and rapid reconfiguration capability to restore service by switching to alternate operational modes or resource allocations.
DP8: Design with built-in testability and self-check mechanisms to enable continuous health verification and early warning of degradation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP6 + DP3 (integrated approach): Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover logic (DP1) coupled with comprehensive fault detection and isolation (DP6) and predictive maintenance framework (DP3) to achieve target availability through prevention, detection, and rapid recovery.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP6 are tightly coupled: failover logic depends on detection signals; detection quality directly impacts failover reliability. Requires explicit interface specification.
DP3 and DP1 have moderate coupling: predictive maintenance informs redundancy strategy (which subsystems to prioritize), but can operate semi-independently.
Risk of over-coupling if redundancy and detection logic are not cleanly separated by module boundaries (IM layer).
Synchronization overhead between redundant subsystems may introduce latency; must verify this does not violate availability measurement window.
coupling_check.notes: The three DPs address complementary failure modes: DP1 handles sudden faults, DP6 enables rapid response, DP3 prevents faults. Coupling is acceptable if interfaces are explicit and testable. Recommend IM decomposition into: (a) Redundancy Manager, (b) Fault Detection Engine, (c) Predictive Analytics Module.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Availability is measured as uptime fraction over evaluation period; risk if measurement window is too short to capture rare failure modes.
Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be synchronized with acceptable latency; risk if synchronization overhead exceeds acceptable bounds.
Assumption: Fault detection has high sensitivity and specificity; risk of false positives triggering unnecessary failovers or false negatives missing real faults.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance data is available and models are accurate; risk if historical data is sparse or operational conditions change significantly.
Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits automated failover without human intervention; risk if safety constraints require manual approval, introducing delay.
Assumption: Critical subsystems can be identified and isolated; risk if failures are systemic or cascade across multiple subsystems.
Assumption: Replacement/repair of failed components can occur within acceptable time window; risk if spare parts or technicians are unavailable.
Assumption: Graceful degradation is acceptable to customer; risk if all functions are equally critical and partial service is unacceptable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'operational availability' (e.g., system ready to accept commands, actively processing, or both); (2) Acceptable downtime per incident and recovery time objective (RTO); (3) Criticality ranking of subsystems and functions; (4) Constraints on automated vs. manual recovery; (5) Measurement methodology and evaluation period duration. Decompose selected DP into three independent IM modules with explicit interfaces: Redundancy Manager (owns failover logic and state synchronization), Fault Detection Engine (owns diagnostics and alerting), Predictive Analytics Module (owns condition monitoring and maintenance scheduling). Establish coupling contract: Detection Engine → Redundancy Manager (fault signals), Predictive Module → Redundancy Manager (maintenance windows), Redundancy Manager → Detection Engine (health queries). Validate that combined MTBF of redundant subsystems and MTTR (mean time to repair) satisfy availability equation: Availability ≈ MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for fault detection, isolation, redundancy management, automatic failover orchestration, and predictive maintenance framework for all critical subsystems across the NGHE solution.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time sensor data, system health metrics, and operational parameters from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to enable fault detection and predictive analysis.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Broadcasts fault detection results, isolation directives, failover triggers, and maintenance recommendations to Vehicle and Teleoperation for corrective action.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational task context to inform predictive maintenance scheduling and failover strategy; sends task suspension/resumption signals during fault recovery.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates redundancy state, failover status, and health telemetry across distributed nodes; receives acknowledgment of failover completion.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural home for fault detection (DP6), failover logic (DP1), and predictive maintenance (DP3). All three DPs are safety-critical and require centralized orchestration. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert/tasking egress needed. Redundancy management and isolation logic remain fully contained.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational subsystem, not a cross-cutting safety orchestrator. Failover logic would diffuse across multiple vehicle components; predictive maintenance would require external visibility into non-vehicle systems (Teleoperation, NGHE infrastructure). Redundancy coordination across Vehicle and Teleoperation cannot be localized here.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control interface, not a fault management layer. Failover and predictive maintenance logic would leak into Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Fault detection requires system-wide visibility that Teleoperation does not naturally possess. Redundancy orchestration across Vehicle and Teleoperation cannot be owned by one of them.

Moderate fit: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container and could theoretically host all three DPs. However, it is overly broad and would blur the distinction between system integration and safety-critical fault management. Safety Services is a more specific, cohesive boundary that isolates safety concerns and improves testability and certification.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive module boundary that contains the integrated DP (DP1 + DP6 + DP3) without significant diffusion. Fault detection, isolation, redundancy failover, and predictive maintenance are all safety-critical concerns that require centralized, cross-system visibility and orchestration. Safety Services naturally owns these responsibilities and exposes them via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking and Network Link for coordination). This boundary minimizes coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation implementations while maintaining clear separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: Redundancy coordination across Vehicle and Teleoperation may require tight coupling to their internal state machines. Mitigation: Define explicit redundancy state contracts (e.g., 'primary', 'standby', 'failed') that Vehicle and Teleoperation expose via Telemetry and respond to via SafetyAlert, avoiding direct internal coupling.

Risk: Predictive maintenance requires deep domain knowledge of Vehicle and Teleoperation failure modes. Mitigation: Establish a predictive maintenance plugin/strategy pattern in Safety Services that allows Vehicle and Teleoperation to register domain-specific health indicators and failure signatures without Safety Services needing to know their internals.

Risk: Failover latency may be unacceptable if Safety Services is remote or network-dependent. Mitigation: Ensure Safety Services can be deployed as a co-located or embedded module with low-latency access to Telemetry and SafetyAlert; consider edge failover logic in Vehicle/Teleoperation that Safety Services coordinates.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if all three DPs (detection, failover, predictive) compete for data. Mitigation: Implement prioritized telemetry streaming and buffering; separate real-time fault detection telemetry from lower-frequency predictive maintenance telemetry.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable availability target (≥0.97 over agreed evaluation period). Safety Services IM boundary provides clear instrumentation points: Telemetry ingress enables continuous monitoring of fault detection effectiveness; SafetyAlert egress enables tracing of failover execution and recovery time; Network Link enables validation of redundancy state synchronization across distributed nodes. Acceptance criteria can be directly mapped to Safety Services observability: (1) fault detection latency, (2) failover completion time, (3) predictive maintenance accuracy (false positive/negative rates), (4) overall system availability computed from incident logs. Test harnesses can inject faults at Telemetry interface and verify SafetyAlert responses and task suspension/resumption behavior.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. The integrated DP (DP1 + DP6 + DP3) is unambiguously allocated to Safety Services. The three constituent DPs are tightly coupled (failover requires detection; predictive maintenance informs failover strategy) and cannot be meaningfully separated without introducing diffusion. Safety Services owns the orchestration logic; Vehicle and Teleoperation own the redundant subsystem implementations and expose health state via Telemetry. The boundary is clear: Safety Services is the decision/coordination layer; Vehicle and Teleoperation are the execution layers. Ownership is unambiguous and avoids the diffusion risks present in Vehicle, Teleoperation, or overly-broad NGHE Solution allocations.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Redundancy state machine coupling. Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose consistent 'primary'/'standby'/'failed' state contracts via Telemetry. If these subsystems implement redundancy state independently (e.g., Vehicle has its own failover logic), Safety Services and Vehicle redundancy logic may conflict or race. Mitigation: Define a canonical redundancy state schema in Safety Services; Vehicle and Teleoperation must report their state to Safety Services, not manage it independently. Safety Services is the single source of truth for failover decisions.

Risk: Predictive maintenance domain knowledge diffusion. Predictive maintenance (DP3) requires understanding failure signatures of Vehicle (e.g., motor degradation, sensor drift) and Teleoperation (e.g., communication latency spikes, control loop instability). If Safety Services hard-codes these signatures, it becomes tightly coupled to Vehicle/Teleoperation implementations and brittle to changes. Mitigation: Implement a plugin/strategy pattern: Vehicle and Teleoperation register health indicator definitions and failure thresholds with Safety Services at initialization. Safety Services applies generic anomaly detection (e.g., statistical deviation, trend analysis) without domain-specific logic.

Risk: Telemetry interface saturation. Fault detection (DP6) requires high-frequency telemetry (e.g., 10 Hz); predictive maintenance (DP3) requires lower-frequency aggregated metrics (e.g., 1 Hz or 1-minute windows). If both compete for the same Telemetry interface, latency-sensitive fault detection may be starved. Mitigation: Implement dual telemetry streams: (1) real-time critical telemetry (sensor readings, subsystem status) prioritized for fault detection; (2) aggregated health telemetry (rolling statistics, trend indicators) for predictive maintenance. Use separate buffers and processing pipelines.

Risk: Failover latency under network partition. If Safety Services is remote and Network Link is severed, failover decisions cannot be communicated to Vehicle/Teleoperation. Mitigation: Deploy Safety Services as a co-located or embedded module with Vehicle and Teleoperation (e.g., on the same edge device or vehicle controller). Implement local failover logic in Vehicle/Teleoperation that Safety Services coordinates; if Safety Services becomes unreachable, Vehicle/Teleoperation fall back to pre-configured failover strategies.

Risk: Circular dependency between Tasking and failover. Tasking sends operational context to Safety Services (to inform predictive maintenance scheduling); Safety Services sends task suspension/resumption signals back via Tasking during failover. If Tasking is blocked or slow, failover coordination may be delayed. Mitigation: Implement asynchronous, non-blocking Tasking interface; use separate channels for task context (infrequent, low-priority) and failover signals (high-priority, time-critical). Ensure failover signals bypass task queuing.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural, cohesive home for the integrated DP. All three constituent DPs (DP1 redundancy/failover, DP6 fault detection, DP3 predictive maintenance) are safety-critical, require cross-system visibility, and must be orchestrated centrally to avoid conflicts and race conditions. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert/tasking egress cross the boundary. Redundancy management, fault isolation logic, and predictive analysis remain fully contained within Safety Services. The module boundary is well-defined and testable. Risks are primarily at the DP<->IM bridge (interface contracts and deployment topology) rather than within the IM itself.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP6 + DP3) is logically sound and directly addresses the FR. FR requires 'operational availability ≥ 0.97 over agreed evaluation period.' DP1 (redundant subsystems + automatic failover) reduces downtime by enabling rapid recovery from single-point failures. DP6 (fault detection and isolation) enables early intervention before failures cascade. DP3 (predictive maintenance) prevents failures proactively. Together, these three DPs form a coherent availability strategy: prevention (DP3) → detection (DP6) → recovery (DP1). The logic is sound: availability = 1 - (downtime / total time). Downtime is minimized by preventing failures (DP3), detecting them quickly (DP6), and recovering rapidly (DP1). The DP is measurable against the FR: availability can be computed from incident logs and recovery times, which are directly observable outputs of Safety Services. No logical gaps or contradictions detected. The DP does not over-specify (e.g., does not mandate specific redundancy topology or predictive algorithm) and allows implementation flexibility while maintaining coherence.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize redundancy state contract. Define a canonical state machine (e.g., 'primary', 'standby', 'degraded', 'failed', 'recovering') that Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose via Telemetry. Document the state transitions and the conditions under which Safety Services triggers failover. This prevents diffusion of redundancy logic across subsystems.

Revision 2: Implement predictive maintenance plugin architecture. Define a registration interface in Safety Services that allows Vehicle and Teleoperation to register health indicators (e.g., 'motor_temperature', 'communication_latency') and failure thresholds without Safety Services needing to know their internals. Safety Services applies generic anomaly detection; subsystems provide domain-specific signatures.

Revision 3: Separate real-time and aggregated telemetry streams. Define two Telemetry sub-interfaces: (1) CriticalTelemetry (high-frequency, prioritized for fault detection); (2) HealthTelemetry (low-frequency, aggregated for predictive maintenance). Use separate buffers and processing pipelines to prevent latency-sensitive fault detection from being starved by predictive maintenance data.

Revision 4: Define failover latency SLA. Specify maximum acceptable latency from fault detection (Telemetry ingress) to failover completion (SafetyAlert egress and task suspension). This drives deployment topology decisions (e.g., co-location, edge failover logic) and acceptance testing criteria.

Revision 5: Clarify Tasking interface priority. Document that failover signals (task suspension/resumption) are high-priority, time-critical, and must not be queued behind operational task context. Consider separate channels or priority levels to ensure failover coordination is not delayed by task scheduling.

Revision 6: Add network partition resilience requirement. Specify that Safety Services must support local failover logic in Vehicle/Teleoperation that operates independently if Network Link is severed. Define the fallback strategy (e.g., pre-configured failover rules, heartbeat-based detection) and the recovery procedure when Safety Services becomes reachable again.

Revision 7: Validate acceptance criteria traceability. Map each acceptance criterion (e.g., 'Measured availability ≥ 0.97') to specific Safety Services observables (e.g., incident logs, recovery times, fault detection latency). Define test cases that exercise each DP (DP1, DP6, DP3) and verify their contribution to the overall availability target.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence, and containment is excellent (0.92). However, the recommended_revisions identify seven critical interface contracts and deployment topology decisions that must be formalized before decomposition can proceed safely. These revisions are not minor clarifications—they address fundamental architectural risks: (1) redundancy state machine coupling between Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation, (2) predictive maintenance domain knowledge diffusion, (3) telemetry interface saturation, (4) failover latency under network partition, and (5) circular dependency between Tasking and failover. Given the high safety criticality of this FR, these risks must be resolved at the current level before deriving sub-FRs. Proceeding to decomposition without formalizing these contracts would propagate ambiguity and coupling risks into lower-level requirements, violating the principle of independence and clarity. The revisions are targeted, actionable, and directly support the DP-IM bridge; they do not require re-scoping the FR or DP.

revise.why: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP6 + DP3) is logically sound, but its realization (IM) requires explicit formalization of interface contracts, state machines, and deployment topology to mitigate diffusion and coupling risks. Revisions 1–7 must be incorporated into the DP specification before decomposition: (1) Formalize redundancy state contract as a canonical state machine exposed by Vehicle and Teleoperation via Telemetry; (2) Implement predictive maintenance as a plugin architecture to prevent domain knowledge diffusion; (3) Separate real-time and aggregated telemetry streams to prevent interface saturation; (4) Define failover latency SLA to drive deployment topology; (5) Clarify Tasking interface priority to prevent circular dependency delays; (6) Add network partition resilience requirement to specify local failover fallback; (7) Validate acceptance criteria traceability to Safety Services observables. These revisions strengthen the DP specification without changing its core logic; they make the DP-IM mapping explicit and testable.

assumptions: Safety Services can be deployed co-located or embedded with Vehicle and Teleoperation to meet failover latency SLA (Revision 4).
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can expose a canonical redundancy state machine via Telemetry without significant re-architecture (Revision 1).
Telemetry infrastructure supports dual streams (real-time critical + aggregated health) with separate buffers and prioritization (Revision 3).
Tasking interface can be modified to support high-priority, non-blocking failover signals separate from operational task context (Revision 5).
Predictive maintenance can be implemented as a generic anomaly detection engine with pluggable health indicator registration (Revision 2).
Network partition resilience can be achieved through pre-configured local failover rules in Vehicle/Teleoperation with Safety Services coordination (Revision 6).
Acceptance criteria (e.g., 'availability ≥ 0.97') can be directly computed from Safety Services incident logs and recovery time observables (Revision 7).
open_risks: Redundancy state machine coupling: Vehicle and Teleoperation may implement independent failover logic conflicting with Safety Services decisions. Mitigation: Formalize canonical state contract (Revision 1); Safety Services is single source of truth.

Predictive maintenance domain knowledge diffusion: Safety Services may hard-code failure signatures, creating tight coupling to Vehicle/Teleoperation implementations. Mitigation: Implement plugin/strategy pattern (Revision 2); subsystems register health indicators at initialization.

Telemetry interface saturation: High-frequency fault detection (10 Hz) and lower-frequency predictive maintenance (1 Hz) may compete for same interface, starving latency-sensitive detection. Mitigation: Separate dual telemetry streams (Revision 3); use distinct buffers and processing pipelines.

Failover latency under network partition: If Safety Services is remote and Network Link is severed, failover decisions cannot be communicated. Mitigation: Deploy Safety Services co-located with Vehicle/Teleoperation; implement local fallback logic (Revision 4, 6).

Circular dependency between Tasking and failover: Tasking may block failover coordination if task context and failover signals share the same queue. Mitigation: Implement asynchronous, non-blocking Tasking interface with separate high-priority failover channel (Revision 5).

Acceptance criteria traceability: Availability target (≥0.97) must be measurable from Safety Services observables; unclear mapping may lead to unverifiable requirements. Mitigation: Formalize traceability to incident logs, recovery times, fault detection latency (Revision 7).
evidence_needed: Formal specification of redundancy state machine (states, transitions, conditions for failover) exposed by Vehicle and Teleoperation via Telemetry (Revision 1).
Design document for predictive maintenance plugin architecture: registration interface, health indicator schema, generic anomaly detection algorithm (Revision 2).
Telemetry interface specification: dual streams (CriticalTelemetry, HealthTelemetry), buffer sizes, prioritization rules, latency SLAs (Revision 3).
Failover latency SLA: maximum acceptable latency from fault detection to failover completion; deployment topology options (co-located, edge, remote) and their latency implications (Revision 4).
Tasking interface specification: priority levels, non-blocking semantics, separate channels for task context vs. failover signals (Revision 5).
Network partition resilience specification: local failover rules in Vehicle/Teleoperation, heartbeat-based detection, recovery procedure when Safety Services becomes reachable (Revision 6).

Acceptance criteria mapping: each criterion (e.g., 'availability ≥ 0.97') linked to specific Safety Services observables (incident logs, recovery times, fault detection latency); test cases for each DP (DP1, DP6, DP3) (Revision 7).
Prototype or proof-of-concept demonstrating dual telemetry streams, plugin-based predictive maintenance, and failover latency under nominal and degraded network conditions.
